section.title: David Delivers Keilah
section.sectionRationale: Still a fugitive with no crown and no safe home, David repeatedly stops to ask the LORD before acting: he rescues the town of Keilah from Philistine raiders, then learns through the ephod that Keilah's citizens would hand him over to Saul. He flees into the wilderness, where Jonathan finds him one last time and strengthens his hand in God with covenant words, before the Ziphites betray his location and Saul's men close in, only to be called away at the last moment by a Philistine raid.
section.thesis: Though hunted, poor, and dependent on the loyalty of towns that might betray him, David consistently inquires of the LORD and obeys what he hears rather than trusting his own instincts or the goodwill of men, and it is the LORD, working even through a surprise enemy raid, who delivers him again and again, while Jonathan's covenant loyalty points him to find his strength in God rather than in circumstances.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: David inquires and delivers Keilah; Saul plots to trap him there and David inquires again about betrayal; Jonathan comes to Horesh and strengthens David's hand in God; the Ziphites betray his hiding place to Saul; and the chase at Maon ends only because the LORD turns Saul aside to meet a Philistine raid.

verses.text: Now it was reported to David, “Look, the Philistines are fighting against Keilah and looting the threshing floors.”
So David {{shaal|inquired}} of the LORD, “Should I go and attack these Philistines?” And the LORD said to David, “Go and attack the Philistines and save Keilah.”
But David's men said to him, “Look, we are afraid here in Judah; how much more if we go to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?”
Once again, David {{shaal|inquired}} of the LORD, and the LORD answered him: “Go at once to Keilah, for I will deliver the Philistines into your {{yad|hand}}.”
Then David and his men went to Keilah, fought against the Philistines, and carried off their livestock, striking them with a mighty blow. So David saved the people of Keilah.
(Now Abiathar son of Ahimelech had brought the ephod with him when he fled to David at Keilah.)

units.label: Saul Plots; David Inquires Again
units.note: will the citizens of Keilah surrender me

verses.text: When Saul was told that David had gone to Keilah, he said, “God has delivered him into my hand, for he has trapped himself by entering a town with gates and bars.”
Then Saul summoned all his troops to go to war at Keilah and besiege David and his men.
When David learned that Saul was plotting evil against him, he said to Abiathar the priest, “Bring the ephod.”
And David said, “O LORD, God of Israel, Your servant has heard that Saul intends to come to Keilah and destroy the city on my account.
Will the citizens of Keilah surrender me into his hand? Will Saul come down, as Your servant has heard? O LORD, God of Israel, please tell Your servant.” “He will,” said the LORD.
So David asked, “Will the citizens of Keilah surrender me and my men into the hand of Saul?” “They will,” said the LORD.
Then David and his men, about six hundred strong, set out and departed from Keilah, moving from place to place. When Saul was told that David had escaped from Keilah, he declined to go forth.

units.label: Jonathan Strengthens David's Hand in God
units.note: you will be king over Israel

verses.text: And David stayed in the wilderness strongholds and in the hill country of the Wilderness of Ziph. Day after day Saul searched for him, but God would not deliver David into his hand.
While David was in Horesh in the Wilderness of Ziph, he saw that Saul had come out to take his life.
And Saul's son Jonathan came to David in Horesh and {{chazaq|strengthened}} his hand in God,
saying, “Do not be afraid, for my father Saul will never lay a hand on you. And you will be king over Israel, and I will be your second-in-command. Even my father Saul knows this is true.”
So the two of them made a covenant before the LORD. And David remained in Horesh, while Jonathan went home.

units.label: The Ziphites Betray David's Hiding Place
units.note: we will be responsible for delivering him

verses.text: Then the Ziphites came up to Saul at Gibeah and said, “Is not David hiding among us in the strongholds at Horesh, on the hill of Hachilah south of Jeshimon?
Now, O king, come down whenever your soul desires, and we will be responsible for delivering him into your hand.”
“May you be blessed by the LORD,” replied Saul, “for you have had compassion on me.
Please go and prepare further. Investigate and watch carefully where he goes and who has seen him there, for I am told that he is extremely cunning.
Observe and find out all the places where he hides. Then come back to me with certainty, and I will go with you. If he is in the land, I will search him out among all the clans of Judah.”

units.label: The Chase at Maon and a Narrow Escape

verses.text: So they set out and went to Ziph ahead of Saul. Now David and his men were in the Wilderness of Maon in the Arabah south of Jeshimon,
and Saul and his men went to seek him. When David was told about it, he went down to the rock and stayed in the Wilderness of Maon. And when Saul heard of this, he pursued David there.

Saul was proceeding along one side of the mountain, and David and his men along the other side. Even though David was hurrying to get away, Saul and his men were closing in on David and his men to capture them.
Then a messenger came to Saul, saying, “Come quickly, for the Philistines have raided the land!”
So Saul broke off his pursuit of David and went to meet the Philistines. That is why that place is called Sela-hammahlekoth.
And David went up from there and lived in the strongholds of En-gedi.

shaal.gloss: To ask, inquire, request. In verses 2 and 4 David inquires of the LORD, almost certainly through the ephod Abiathar carries, seeking a clear answer before he risks his men's lives against the Philistines at Keilah. The repeated inquiry frames David's fugitive life as one of humble dependence rather than self-direction.

yad.gloss: Hand, power, control. In verse 4 the LORD promises to deliver the Philistines into David's hand; the same word marks the danger of David falling into Saul's hand elsewhere in the chapter, verses 7, 11-12, 14, framing the whole narrative as a contest over whose hand actually controls David's fate.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, to strengthen. In verse 16 Jonathan strengthens David's hand in God, not in Jonathan himself, in weapons, or in political scheming; the phrase names God as the true source of courage and endurance for the hunted king.

basic.context: David, already a fugitive with a band of about four hundred, soon six hundred, men, hears that the Philistines are raiding the Israelite town of Keilah. Rather than assume this is his chance to be a hero, or ignore it as someone else's problem, he inquires of the LORD, through the ephod Abiathar has brought him, twice, because his own men are afraid. He obeys, defeats the Philistines, and saves the town. But when Saul hears David is holed up in a walled town, he sees an opportunity to trap him. David inquires again and learns, to his own men's horror, that the very town he just rescued would hand him over to save themselves. He flees into the wilderness of Ziph, where Jonathan makes one final visit, encouraging David not with a rescue plan but with a reminder that God is with him, and the two renew their covenant. The Ziphites then betray David's location for Saul's favor, and Saul closes in at Maon, until, in a moment of stunning providence, a Philistine raid pulls Saul away just before he can capture David.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, David's own experience during his fugitive years, not a formula guaranteeing that obedience always means comfort or safety. It does establish an enduring pattern worth imitating, seek God's guidance before acting, keep seeking it even when the answer is unwelcome, and trust Him even when human loyalty fails and danger closes in.
basic.originalLanguages: Twice, verses 2 and 4, David inquires, sha'al, of the LORD before acting, refusing to let even his men's legitimate fear override seeking God's will first. The LORD answers plainly that He will deliver the Philistines into David's hand, yad, showing whose hand truly controls outcomes even while Saul's hand, and the Ziphites' hands, threaten David elsewhere in the chapter. And when the danger is greatest, Jonathan comes and does not offer soldiers or supplies but strengthens, chazaq, David's hand in God, pointing him to the true source of courage.
basic.typology: David's pattern of inquiring of the LORD before every major decision, even ones with willing armies and clear military logic behind them, foreshadows Christ's own dependence on the Father, who said He does nothing on His own but only what He sees the Father doing. Jonathan's covenant loyalty that strengthens another's hand in God, asking nothing for himself, dimly pictures the self-giving love of the true covenant Friend, who lays down His life for His friends.
basic.christ: David will not act, even to rescue a town under attack, without first seeking the LORD's word, and he keeps seeking it even when the answer exposes painful truths about those he has just saved. This dependence anticipates the Son who said, I can do nothing on My own, as I hear, I judge, always moving in step with the Father's will rather than by His own initiative. And when David is most exposed, hunted by the king and betrayed by the town he saved, Jonathan comes not to rescue him by force but to strengthen his hand in God, a faint shadow of the greater Friend who lays down His life for those He loves and who is Himself our strength when every human support fails.
basic.god: God is the hidden engineer of the whole chapter. He answers David's inquiries with plain, specific direction, He exposes Keilah's coming betrayal before it can happen, and He moves Jonathan to encourage David at exactly the moment despair might have won. Above all, when Saul's men are closing in and capture seems certain, it is the LORD who sends the Philistines raiding at precisely the right hour, turning Saul aside without David lifting a hand. Nothing in the chapter happens by luck, every deliverance is traced, directly or by the narrative's timing, to God's governing hand.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 30 shows David continuing this same pattern of inquiring of the LORD through the ephod at Ziklag. Psalm 54 is traditionally tied to the Ziphites' betrayal here. John 5 shows Christ's pattern of acting only according to the Father's will. John 15 gives the pattern of laying down one's life for one's friends, which Jonathan's loyalty faintly anticipates.
basic.oneStory: In the long years before his throne, David learns to live by asking and obeying rather than by his own strength or the loyalty of men, and time and again it is the LORD who delivers him, whether through victory at Keilah, warning of betrayal, Jonathan's timely encouragement, or a providential Philistine raid. This waiting, inquiring king points toward the greater Son of David who did only what He saw the Father doing, and whose friends find their truest strength not in circumstances but in Him.

context.literary: The chapter is built from three rescue-and-threat cycles, Keilah delivered yet nearly betraying David, Ziph where Jonathan's covenant visit is immediately undercut by the Ziphites' betrayal, and Maon where capture seems certain until providence intervenes. The repeated inquiries of the LORD, verses 2, 4, 10-12, frame David's decisions, and the chapter's tension is resolved not by David's cunning but by circumstances entirely outside his control.
context.historical: Keilah was a fortified town in the Judean lowlands vulnerable to Philistine raiding on threshing floors at harvest time. A walled town like Keilah, prized for its gates and bars, also made an attractive trap in Saul's eyes, since a siege could pin David down. The Ziphites lived in the wilderness of Judah south of Hebron and had reason to curry favor with Saul rather than shelter a fugitive, however unjustly hunted.
context.canonical: This chapter continues the fugitive narrative that occupies much of 1 Samuel 19 through 27, and it supplies the setting Jewish and Christian tradition connect to the superscriptions of Psalms 54 and 63. David's habit of inquiring of the LORD through Abiathar's ephod recurs at Ziklag, 1 Samuel 30:7-8, forming a pattern that marks his reign even after he becomes king.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show David's fugitive years as a school of dependence on the LORD's guidance rather than self-reliance, and to demonstrate that the LORD, not human loyalty or David's own strategy, is the one who actually delivers him from every threat.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes David's specific circumstances, an ephod, a wilderness, particular enemies, which are not the ordinary means believers today have for guidance. What endures is the pattern of habitually seeking God before acting, trusting Him even when the truth is painful, such as Keilah's coming betrayal, and drawing strength from covenant relationships that point us to God rather than replace dependence on Him.

debates.question: Why did David inquire of the LORD a second time in verses 10-12 rather than trusting his first success at Keilah?

views.label: Genuine humility that never assumes past guidance covers future decisions
views.case: Each situation, defeating the Philistines and then discerning Keilah's loyalty, was a distinct question; David treats obedience as an ongoing posture rather than a one-time transaction, inquiring afresh when the situation changed.

views.label: Practical wisdom prompted by his men's fear and the stakes of a siege
views.case: David's men had already voiced fear in verse 3; with six hundred lives and Saul's whole army involved, prudence alone would call for confirming the danger before committing to defend a walled town that could become a trap.

debates.bearing: Either way, the chapter commends returning to God for fresh guidance rather than coasting on past answers, especially when the stakes and circumstances have changed.

debates.question: Would the men of Keilah really have betrayed their rescuer?

views.label: Yes, self-preservation before an army would override gratitude
views.case: The LORD's own answer, they will, verse 12, states it plainly; facing Saul's full military threat, a small town would very likely surrender a fugitive guest rather than face destruction, regardless of the debt they owed him.

views.label: The prophecy describes what would happen only if David stayed
views.case: Like other conditional prophetic warnings in Scripture, the LORD's answer describes the certain outcome of a particular course of events; by leaving, David rendered the betrayal moot, so the town's actual future choice is never tested.

debates.bearing: Either way, the episode teaches David, and us, not to presume on gratitude or loyalty as a substitute for seeking God's guidance about real danger.

study.originalLanguages: David's fugitive wisdom is carried in a handful of repeated words. Twice he sha'al, inquires, of the LORD, verses 2 and 4, refusing to act on instinct or his men's fear alone. The LORD answers by promising to deliver the Philistines into David's yad, hand, the same word that marks the danger of Saul's hand and the Ziphites' hands closing in on David later in the chapter, so that the narrative keeps asking whose hand will finally hold David's fate. And when despair might easily have won, at Horesh, Jonathan comes not with an army but with words that chazaq, strengthen, David's hand in God, teaching that ultimate courage is found not in circumstances or allies but in the LORD Himself.

typology.type: David repeatedly inquiring of the LORD before acting
typology.fulfillment: Christ's total dependence on the Father, doing nothing on His own but only what He sees the Father doing, John 5:19-30.

typology.type: Jonathan strengthening David's hand in God through costly covenant loyalty
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Friend, who lays down His life for His friends and is Himself our strength, John 15:13-15.

typology.type: The LORD delivering David through a providential Philistine raid rather than David's own strength
typology.fulfillment: God's pattern of rescuing His people by His own initiative and timing, not by human strategy, ultimately displayed in the resurrection.

study.christ: David will not act, even to rescue a town under attack, without first seeking the LORD's word, and he keeps seeking it even when the answer exposes painful truths about those he has just saved. This dependence anticipates the Son who said, I can do nothing on My own, as I hear, I judge, John 5:30, always moving in step with the Father's will rather than by His own initiative. And when David is most exposed, hunted by the king and betrayed by the town he saved, Jonathan comes not to rescue him by force but to strengthen his hand in God, a faint shadow of the greater Friend who lays down His life for those He loves, John 15:13, and who is Himself our strength when every human support fails.
study.god: God is the hidden engineer of the whole chapter. He answers David's inquiries with plain, specific direction, He exposes Keilah's coming betrayal before it can happen, and He moves Jonathan to encourage David at exactly the moment despair might have won. Above all, when Saul's men are closing in and capture seems certain, it is the LORD who sends the Philistines raiding at precisely the right hour, turning Saul aside without David lifting a hand. Nothing in the chapter happens by luck, every deliverance is traced, directly or by the narrative's careful timing, to God's governing hand over kings, armies, and even foreign raiders.

crossReferences.note: David's ongoing pattern of inquiring of the LORD through the ephod before major decisions, continued later at Ziklag.
Traditionally connected to the Ziphites' betrayal of David's hiding place in this chapter.
Christ's own pattern of acting only according to the Father's will, echoing David's dependence on inquiring of the LORD.
Greater love has no one than this, that he lay down his life for his friends, the pattern Jonathan's costly loyalty faintly anticipates.

study.oneStory: In the long years before his throne, David learns to live by asking and obeying rather than by his own strength or the loyalty of men, and time and again it is the LORD who delivers him, whether through victory at Keilah, warning of betrayal, Jonathan's timely encouragement, or a providential Philistine raid arriving at the exact moment of greatest danger. This waiting, inquiring king points toward the greater Son of David, who did only what He saw the Father doing, and whose friends find their truest strength not in circumstances but in Him alone.

quiz.q: Why did David hesitate to attack the Philistines at Keilah until inquiring of the LORD?

options.text: He inquired of the LORD, and when his men remained afraid, he inquired again to be sure before committing.
options.note: Verses 2 and 4 show David seeking and receiving explicit guidance before acting, even after an initial answer.

options.text: He was cautious because his men were frightened of facing the Philistine army.
options.note: The men's fear is named in verse 3, but the passage's emphasis is that David sought the LORD's word first, not merely that he shared their fear.

options.text: He needed more soldiers before he could safely engage the Philistines.
options.note: The text does not mention troop numbers as the issue; the concern was seeking God's direction and his men's fear.

options.text: He refused to fight until Saul gave him permission.
options.note: Saul was David's pursuer, not his commander; David sought the LORD's permission, not Saul's.

quiz.q: What did David learn when he inquired of the LORD a second time about Keilah, in verses 10-12?

options.text: That the citizens of Keilah, whom he had just saved, would surrender him into Saul's hand.
options.note: Verses 11-12 give the LORD's plain answers, he will come down, and they will surrender you.

options.text: That Saul was gathering troops to besiege Keilah.
options.note: True and already reported in verses 7-8, but the new information from this second inquiry was Keilah's coming betrayal.

options.text: That the Philistines would attack Keilah again.
options.note: The chapter does not mention a second Philistine attack; the danger David learns of is Saul and Keilah's betrayal.

options.text: That the LORD refused to answer him a second time.
options.note: The LORD answered both inquiries clearly, in verses 11 and 12.

quiz.q: How did Jonathan strengthen David's hand in verses 16-18?

options.text: He came to Horesh, reminded David that he would be king and that Saul knew it, and renewed their covenant, pointing David to find courage in God.
options.note: Verses 16-17 explicitly say Jonathan strengthened his hand in God, followed by a covenant in verse 18.

options.text: He brought soldiers to help David fight off Saul's pursuit.
options.note: Jonathan brought only words of covenant encouragement, not an armed force; he went home afterward, verse 18.

options.text: He persuaded Saul to call off the pursuit entirely.
options.note: Saul's pursuit continues after this scene; it is a Philistine raid later, not Jonathan, that turns Saul away.

options.text: He urged David to surrender and make peace with Saul.
options.note: The opposite is true, Jonathan affirms that David will be king and that Saul cannot succeed against him.

quiz.q: How did the Ziphites treat David?

options.text: They betrayed his hiding place to Saul, offering to help deliver him into Saul's hand.
options.note: Verses 19-20 record their report to Saul and their offer to help capture David.

options.text: They ignored David and stayed neutral in the conflict.
options.note: The text shows active betrayal, not mere neutrality; they proactively sought out Saul with information.

options.text: They tried to warn David that Saul was coming.
options.note: The opposite is true; they informed on David to Saul, not the reverse.

options.text: They joined David's band of six hundred men.
options.note: They sided with Saul against David, offering to hand him over.

quiz.q: What finally interrupted Saul's pursuit at Maon just as he was closing in on David?

options.text: A messenger reported a Philistine raid on the land, and Saul broke off pursuit to meet them.
options.note: Verses 27-28 record this providential interruption at the exact moment of danger.

options.text: David's men managed to outrun and outmaneuver Saul's forces.
options.note: The text credits the Philistine raid, not David's own tactics, with the escape; Saul's men were actually closing in, verse 26.

options.text: Jonathan intervened again to plead for David's life.
options.note: Jonathan's visit was earlier in the chapter, at Horesh; a Philistine raid, not Jonathan, saved David at Maon.

options.text: Saul simply gave up the chase out of exhaustion.
options.note: Saul broke off specifically because of the Philistine threat, not fatigue or a change of heart.

questions: David inquired of the LORD before rescuing Keilah, and again before trusting Keilah's loyalty. Where in your life do you act on your own instincts when you should pause and seek God's guidance first?

The town David rescued was ready to betray him for its own safety. Have you experienced, or been tempted toward, that kind of self-protective betrayal, and how does it shape your view of human loyalty?

Jonathan came to strengthen David's hand in God, not to offer an escape plan. Who in your life points you back to God rather than merely trying to fix your circumstances, and are you that kind of friend to someone else?
Saul's pursuit was broken off by a sudden, unplanned Philistine raid, not by David's cleverness. Where have you seen God use unexpected circumstances to rescue you when you had no way out yourself?

David kept seeking God's direction even after receiving painful answers, like Keilah's coming betrayal. How do you respond when God's guidance reveals something hard rather than something comfortable?

translationNotes.note: This verse is a narrator's parenthetical aside explaining how Abiathar came to have the ephod with him, connecting back to his escape in chapter 22 and enabling David's repeated inquiries of the LORD in this chapter.

The Wilderness of Ziph names the rugged hill country south of Hebron where David hid; the phrase God would not deliver David into his hand answers directly to the earlier the LORD will deliver into your hand of verse 4, contrasting whose hand truly controls events.
Sela-hammahlekoth, footnoted as Rock of Escape, preserves in its very name the memory of this providential rescue, marking the site where Saul's pursuit was broken off.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 23:1-29. No em or en dashes appear in the source or in this file; hyphenated place and compound words, Sela-hammahlekoth, En-gedi, second-in-command, and eighty-five in the companion chapter, are hyphens, not dashes.
